title: Throughput

content.statement: Speed of output matters more than effort.
content.question: Where can you double throughput by removing one step, not trying harder?
sop: Time how long one full cycle of output takes, start to finish.
Calculate current Throughput, Output divided by Time (see calculation, page 6).
Remove or combine steps that don't add value to the final output.
Batch similar tasks instead of switching between different types of work.
Re-time the cycle after changes to confirm throughput actually increased.
questions: Where can you double throughput by removing one step, not trying harder?
How long does one full cycle of your work actually take, start to finish, and have you ever timed it?
What step in your process exists mostly to switch between two different kinds of work?

definitions.def: The rate of output per unit of time.

content.equation_example: You complete 12 client projects, Output, in 30 days, Time. Throughput equals 12 divided by 30 = 0.4 projects per day. A higher number means you're producing faster without necessarily working longer; a falling number even with the same hours worked means something in the process is slowing you down.
content.closing_statement: Removing a step beats trying to do it faster.

caption:
Principle Nº 119: Throughput

Speed of output matters more than effort.

Ask yourself: Where can you double throughput by removing one step, not trying harder?
